coredata.dc:title: A microlagal-based carbonaceous sensor for enzymatic determination of glucose in blood serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Seyed Mostafa
Masoum, Saeed
Tafreshi, Seyed Ali Hosseini
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, Chlorella vulgaris, a well-known microalga was used as a carbonic precursor, to synthesis the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) via hydrothermal method. To improve the properties, acidic carbon dots (ACDs) were also synthesized via ultrasoncation of microalgal biomass after acidic hydrolysis (using diluted acidic solution). The aqueous solution of ACDs was used as a sensor for glucose determination based on glucose oxidase enzyme (GOx) reaction and fluorescence quenching of ACDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions under the optimized conditions. The linear range of glucose in the blood serum was measured from a range of 5 µM to 500 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) 2.84 µM. Additionally, the hydrogen peroxide, as the conventional reactive oxygen species (ROS) was determined based on the Fenton reaction. The linear range of H2O2 was obtained from 10 µM to 200 µM with LOD of 975 nM. In all of these applications, fluorescence quenching of ACDs was followed according to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism in the presence of generated Fe3+ ions by Fenton reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonaceous sensor
Microalga
Carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching
Glucose
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the ACDs for glucose detection in the presence of glucose oxidase enzyme (GOx) and Fe2+ ions
Spectral characteristics of synthesized CDs and ACDs
Effect of H2O2/Fe2+ molar ratio and time on the Fenton reaction
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs
Fluorescence assay of ACDs and studying interference effect of some metal ions and compounds (biomolecules)
Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor
Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor for Fe3+ ions detection
Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor for H2O2 determination
Analytical performance of the sensor for determination of glucose in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The microalgae (Chlorella vulgaris) green solution, (b) This image shows distribution of microalgae particles under a typical microscope, (c) precipitated and dried microalgae, (d) powdering microalgae of dried one, (e) the microalgae in deionized water and stirring for swelling and preparation of microalgae for use in hydrothermal, (f) and (g) show the bright brown ACDs concentrate solution and pale yellow ACDs dilute solution under day-light, respectively, (h) ACDs dilute solution under UV-light (365 nm), (i) a residual precipitation of precursor after hydrothermal and centrifuge steps.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (slit ex:em (nm); 15:3), (b) fluorescence spectra of the ACDs (slit ex:em (nm); 15:3). Insets: CDs and ACDs solutions under day-light (A-tagged, left) and ultraviolet light (B-tagged, right) under a 365 nm UV lamp.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the ACDs (c) TEM image for ACDs (d) FT-IR spectra for microalga, CDs and ACDs dry powders.

(a) Study of fluorescence quenching mechanism of ACDs (concentration of H2O2 is 50 μΜ and other concentrations are 100 μΜ) (b) the effect of two different buffers (PB and Tris, 0.2 M) on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs from pH 2–11, (c) metal ions interference effect at the fluorescence quenching of ACDs in comprison to the blank, ([Metal ions] = 500 µM), (d) the influence of the some compounds (such as: SC, KCl, AA, LA, GL, UA, [compounds] = 100 µM) on the ACDs fluorescence intensity in the presence of the GOx enzyme and Fe2+ ion. In b, c and d diagrams, the error bars were calculated from the responses of three independent experiment.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of the ACDs by addition of Fe3+ ions, (b) the regression curve for decreased intensity of ACDs as function of Fe3+ ions concentration, (c) fluorescence spectra of the ACDs in the presence of the various concentrations of H2O2/Fe2+ (molar ratio is 1:2, in all over steps), (d) linear plot of the decreased intensity of ACDs fluorescence (F0 − F) as funtion of the H2O2 concentration, (e) gradual decrease of the ACDs fluorescence intensity with gradual increase of glucose concentration (5–900 µM), (f) calibration curve of fluorescence quenching (F0 − F) as a function of the various glucose concentrations. Inset in (f): the linear range of glucose calibration curve in the range of 5 µM to 500 µM.

(a) Schematic pathway for synthesis of ACDs and their application as a glucose sensor (b) two pathways for ACDs fluorescence emission and quenching mechanism: (1) fluorescence process, (2) PET quenching mechanism for ACDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions.
Analysis of glucose in serum sample by proposed sensor and laboratory assay.
Comparison of the developed probe for glucose detection with other probes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reduced graphene oxide/ PAMAM(dendrimer)- silver nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: A microlagal-based carbonaceous sensor for enzymatic determination of glucose in blood serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cell and Molecular Biology, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Cell and Molecular Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, Chlorella vulgaris, a well-known microalga was used as a carbonic precursor, to synthesis the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) via hydrothermal method. To improve the properties, acidic carbon dots (ACDs) were also synthesized via ultrasoncation of microalgal biomass after acidic hydrolysis (using diluted acidic solution). The aqueous solution of ACDs was used as a sensor for glucose determination based on glucose oxidase enzyme (GOx) reaction and fluorescence quenching of ACDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions under the optimized conditions. The linear range of glucose in the blood serum was measured from a range of 5 µM to 500 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) 2.84 µM. Additionally, the hydrogen peroxide, as the conventional reactive oxygen species (ROS) was determined based on the Fenton reaction. The linear range of H2O2 was obtained from 10 µM to 200 µM with LOD of 975 nM. In all of these applications, fluorescence quenching of ACDs was followed according to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism in the presence of generated Fe3+ ions by Fenton reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon materials were used in the numerous fields of sciences. Carbon dots (CDs), as nanoparticles with the size of less than 10 nm, have previously been proposed as a fascinating class of zero-dimensional, quasi-spherical, carbonaceous photonic-nanomaterials [1–6]. Furthermore, CDs have several useful properties, such as having a low toxicity, reliable stability, high quantum yield, and great selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy that make them as the promising materials for drug delivery [7], or imaging and biosensing systems [8]. The CDs consist of mainly of sp2 hybrid conjugated carbons as the core, and the shell of organic functional groups (e.g., N–H, CO, –OH, –COOH, C–N, C–O) or polymer aggregates [9]. Carbon dots can be synthesized using the natural or chemical reagents as precursors. Also, due to intrinsic properties of CDs such as, proper size and wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL), low photo-bleaching, excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and environmental friendly, these materials could be applied satisfactory in bioimaging [8], sensing [10], biosensing [11], and photo-dynamic therapy [12].

In addition, due to the favorable hydrophilicity [21], and good water-solubility of CDs [22], these nanomaterials were utilized in sensing of some biomolecules such as glucose [23–26], human α-Fetoprotein [27], DNA [28] and metal ions sensor like Fe3+ [21,29], Cr6+ [30,31], Ag+ [32], Cu2+ [33], Zn2+ [34], Mn2+ [35] and Hg2+ [36].

Diabetes is considered as a glucose metabolic disorder arising from insulin deficiency or resistance [37]. The effective and accurate determination of blood glucose (sugar) in human diabetics is an important issue in the clinical field [37]. Thus, numerous researches have focused their investigations to optimize the methods for blood glucose determination. In recent years, different techniques have been developed for sensing of glucose, such as chemiluminescence [38], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [39], mass spectrometry [40], colorimetry [41], electrochemistry [42], and fluorescence spectroscopy [43].

Microalgae are among the most promising species for production of valuable compounds and have a vast range of biotechnological applications. Chlorella vulgaris, as one of the most well-known microalgal genus, is a unicellular organism that lives in fresh water and can produce a range of bioactive compounds such as proteins, vitamins, pigments, fatty acids, sterols and others [44]. The biomass of C. vulgaris predominantly contains 55.79% of proteins, 10.97% of carbohydrate, 6.34% of lipid and 26.9% other materials [45]. Therefore, this carbon- and nitrogen-rich material has been proposed as an excellent candidate for synthesis of CDs [46].

At the present study, ACDs were synthesized from C. vulgaris biomass in a straightforward designed procedure. Then, the performance of ACDs, as a platform was studied for detection of H2O2 and Fe3+ in an aqueous solution based on the Fenton reaction. In the following, synthesized ACDs were applied to develop a sensitive and selective probe for the detection of human serum glucose based on the Fenton reaction and GOx enzyme assisted.

In this research, ultrasonic-assisted acid hydrolysis was performed as an efficient technique (without the usage of organic solvents) for pretreatment of C. vulgaris microalgal as a natural, edible and safe material in the synthesis of ACDs. In addition, by this pretreatment the fluorescence quantum yield of ACDs was increased (about 2.5 times compared to the pretreatment without acidic solution). It seems that the used technique plays a significant role in pre-processing of precursor for ACDs synthesis. Finally, ACDs were applied as a fluorescence platform for glucose sensing. Due to the possess of safety, synthesized ACDs can be a motivation for sensor design of intracellular glucose detection in living cells in future developed works.

Quinine sulfate was purchased from Aladdin Industry Company, Glucose oxidase enzyme (50 KU/g, type X-S from Aspergillus niger) was prepared from Sigma (USA), Chlorella vulgaris was prepared from UTEX culture collection of algae at the University of Texas (Austin, USA). All substances (metal salts, acids and consume compounds) were purchased from Merck company. A standard serum (as the normal control contained 95 mg/dl of glucose) was purchased from Tehran Farasa Mehr company for calibration of BT 3000 autoanalyzer instrument. A FAPAN ultrasound 400 UT instrument (Iran) was used for ultrasonicaion of samples as ultrasonic homogenizer probe. For purification and separation of mixtures, PIT UNIVERSAL 320 centrifuge instrument (Iran) was applied. A Zeiss EM10C transmission electron microscope (TEM, Germany) operating at 100 kV was used for morphology studying of synthesized ACDs. An image was prepared as follows: The dilute aqueous solution of the ACDs was sonicated for 10 min (with cycle 30 kHz and power 50 W). Then, a portion of it (20 μL) was dropped onto a carbon film on a copper grid 300 mesh (EMS-USA) and dried thoroughly at the room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of microalga, synthesized CDs, and ACDs were obtained on a Magna 550 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolt, USA) by using KBr plate. The photoluminescence spectra were carried out with a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured with an OPTIZEN 3220UV spectrometer (South Korea). The purification of synthesized CDs was carried out by dialysis membrane (Sigma-Aldrich, 14 kDa).

The C. vulgaris alga was cultivated in the Bold Basal medium under a controlled condition, at 25 °C and 100 μmol photons m−2 s−1 under a light/dark cycle of 12:12 h. The culture with an optical density (OD680) of around 2 was harvested by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. After washing with distilled water and centrifugation as before, the resulting pellet dried in an oven at 50 °C until reaching constant weight. The dried algal biomass was ground and powdered in the mortar and pestle before using for CDs synthesis.

The CDs were green synthesized from algal powder, as a natural carbon precursor in a two-step procedure. In the first step, 0.3 g of microalgal powder was poured in a 20-ml beaker contained 6 mL deionized water and stirred around 1 hour to swelling of powder. The mixture, was then decanted to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at optimized temperature and time (220 °C, 12 h, respectively). The hydrothermal method has been widely applied because of its simple procedure, low cost and green properties [47]. Teflon reactor was cooled to the room temperature. Subsequently, by-product blackish mixture was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min. Then, upper yellowish solution was filtered by a filter-paper (0.22 μm) and dialysis using a bag (MWCO 14 kDa) against deionized water for 48 h to remove residual material and large size from CDs nanoparticles. At the second step, during the time of microalgal swelling, ultrasonic waves were applied for 10 min. A solution with the mixture of HCl/H2SO4 (3:1), (with final concentration of about 3% and 1% (w/w) in the mixture for HCl and H2SO4, respectively) was added into the beaker and the mixture was stirred around 2 h at the room temperature (with constant stirring) until acidic hydrolysis of microalgae was completed. This mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon reactor and was kept at an optimized temperature and time (220 °C, 12 h, respectively). The Teflon reactor was then cooled up to the room temperature and black color suspension product was centrifuged (9000 rpm, 10 min) for separation of large-size particles and black precipitation of upper yellowish solution. The yellowish acidic CDs solution was purified by dialysis bag (MWCO 14 kDa) for 48 h as mentioned above for CDs purification. At this stage, the obtained CDs were named to “ACDs”. The above-mentioned procedure is presented in Scheme 1 a. The procedure for obtaining the green powder of microalga, swelling of microalga in water, and getting final products through hydrothermal method is also demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

The synthesize of ACDs was proposed due to the following reasons: (1) the fluorescence of the CDs (that were synthesized only from microalga) was quenched in the presence of Fe2+/Fe3+ ions by the static-quenching mechanism [48]; (2) the pH of the CDs solution was about 7.0. Thus, under conditions of basic or neutral pH, Fe2+ or Ni2+ can be combined to –NH groups for complex formation via static-quenching mechanism [48]. On the other hand, we tend that CDs can be participated in the Fenton reaction. However, the fluorescence of synthesized CDs, probably due to the presence of amino groups on the surface of CDs, was quenched in the presence of Fe2+ ions. Acidic hydrolysis of microalga (before of hydrothermal step) not only caused to increase of the carboxylic acid groups on the CDs surface, but the acidic solution of ACDs (about pH 4) also prevented the complexation of Fe2+ ions and residual –NH groups on the ACDs surface. Besides, the acidic hydrolysis of microalga was used to degrade starch and cellulose to reduce sugar [49] and this approach could enhance the efficiency of ACDs production. So, the quantum yield of the prepared ACDs was higher than the quantum yield of CDs, thereby, ACDs were selected as a sensitive platform to achieve the desired goals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the ACDs for glucose detection in the presence of glucose oxidase enzyme (GOx) and Fe2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The protocol for glucose determination was performed in three steps. Step 1: GOx enzyme specifically catalyzed the oxidation of glucose in the presence of oxygen and H2O to form H2O2 and gluconic acid in Tris buffer solution (pH = 7.0, 0.05 M) [41,50]. The incubation time (30 min) for this step was obtained through the optimization of conditions. Step 2: 100 µM of Fe2+ solution was added to preceding solution to react with H2O2 and generate Fe+3 ions in solution (adjusted in pH = 4 by using HCl 1 M). In this stage, the incubated time was about 10 min. Step 3: the ACDs solution (100 μL of stock solution) was added to the above solution. Then, water was added until the total volume reached to 5 mL. After that, the obtained solution was incubated for 5 min at the room temperature and its fluorescence spectra were recorded and compared to that of an ACDs solution spectrum as blank. In practice, the difference between the fluorescence intensity of the ACDs before (F0) and after (F) addition of the Fe2+ solution was determined as ΔF (ΔF = F0 − F) that represented the fluorescence quenching of ACDs due to interaction with Fe3+ ions. Indeed, ΔF explained quenching efficiency that was calculated using Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q]) [13]. The calculated ΔF was then plotted versus the various concentrations of glucose and a linear equation was obtained from the calibration plot. For calculation of limit of detection (LOD), fluorescence spectra of ACDs solution (as blank) were obtained in 10 replications (N = 10) and the intensity of fluorescence spectra was calculated by fluorescence instrument software. Whereas, the catalytic activity of GOx was pH-dependent, the neutral environment of this step (pH = 7.0, Tris buffer 0.05 M) was in favor of H2O2 generation. An acidic environment (pH 4, for example) was suitable for generation of Fe3+ ions according to the Fenton reaction. Thereby, the fluorescence spectrum of ACDs had the maximum intensity and sensitivity under explained conditions.

Blood samples were simply and manually taken from the fasting volunteers (between 9 and 12 h) by syringe and were poured in the separate vials. After half an hour, each vial containing the blood sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 3500 rpm and the upper yellowish solution (as serum) was separated from the red sediment. Then, the serum samples from healthy and diabetic volunteers were kept at −20 °C. Prior glucose analysis test, the frozen serum samples were placed at the room temperature to form a liquid serum again. Finally, these serum vials were shaken several times to homogenate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characteristics of synthesized CDs and ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs and ACDs were soluble in water and were brownish and bright brown in color under day-light, respectively. On the other hand, the colors of both dots were turned into bright blue under UV-light (365 nm). The photoluminescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths (with 10 nm increment) were scanned using spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence of CDs and ACDs showed a wavelength-dependent property. By increasing of the excitation wavelengths, all of the fluorescence peaks red shifted towards longer wavelengths. Fig. 2 a and b indicate the maximum fluorescence emission intensity of CDs and ACDs at 409 nm (with λ ex = 330 nm), and 447 nm (with λ ex = 380 nm), respectively. At excitation wavelengths longer than the maximum ones (λ ex,max), fluorescence peaks intensity decreased gradually and red shift took place. According to a previous study, emissive behavior of the CDs is immensely dependent on their surface functionalities [51]. It has also been reported that surface defects that are primarily created through surface oxidation, served as the capture centers for exciton, thus giving rise to surface-state-related fluorescence [52]. Hence, it could be suggested that with increasing of fluorescence red shift, both the carboxyl contents and oxidation degrees of the surface synthesized ACDs increased more [52] compared to synthesized CDs. For study the absorption properties of CDs and ACDs, the absorption wavelengths were scanned at 200–450 nm for both samples (Fig. 3 a and b). The UV–Vis analysis of CDs indicated two peaks at λ max 205 nm (π-π* transition, CC group), and 265 nm (n-π* transition, CO group). The maximum wavelengths of ACDs were shifted towards longer wavelengths (25 nm and 15 nm for π-π* transition and n-π* transition, respectively), due to surface passivation of CDs.

The quantum yields of CDs and ACDs were measured against quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54), as a standard [53]. The quantum yields of CDs and ACDs were obtained 4.36% and 10.44%, respectively (Fig. S1). Because the QY of ACDs was better than that of CDs, the ACDs were employed in the following experiments.
The TEM image of ACDs is shown in Fig. 3c. As indicated, the diameters of these spherical nanoparticles were less than 10 nm.

FT-IR spectra of microalgal biomass, and synthesized CDs, and ACDs demonstrated the presence of surface functional groups (Fig. 3d). According to the spectrum analysis, some of the prominent bands were: (1) the IR band at around 1600 cm−1 showed the CC (stretching vibration) and bending vibration of –NH group; (2) the IR band at around 1100 cm−1 that indicated bending vibration of C–O group, was correlated to alcoholic (C–OH) or carboxylic acid (–COOH) group; (3) the IR bands (stretching vibrations) between 800–900 cm−1 and 1400–1430 cm−1 related to alkenic (or vinylic) hydrogen (C–H); (4) a stretching vibration at about 3400 cm−1 was attributed to hydroxyl or carboxyl groups and presence of –NH(amidic) on the CDs surfaces. Meanwhile, the excellent water solubility of CDs and ACDs indicated that hydrophilic groups were relatively distributed on the surface of carbon dots.

According to the previous reports [54,55], Fe3+ and OH
can quench the fluorescence of ACDs based on the Fenton reaction (Mn+ + H2O2→ Mn+1 + OH
+ OH−). Therefore, two methods were used in the present study to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In this context, the Co2+ and Ni2+ ions were used to replace Fe2+ ion for catalyzing H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals. This experiment was performed as the following steps: (1) 100 μL of stock solution of ACDs was diluted in 5 mL volumetric flask using deionized water and its fluorescence spectrum at λ ex = 380 nm was recorded as blank; (2) the solutions of Fe2+, Co2+ and Ni2+ (with the final concentration of 100 μM) in presence of 100 μL of ACDs solution was prepared in 5 mL volumetric flask, separately. Each solutions was vortexed for 10 min and their fluorescence spectra were recorded; (3) the solutions of Fe2+, Co2+ and Ni2+ (with the final concentration of 100 μM) in the presence of 100 μL of ACDs solution and H2O2 (100 μL, 10 mM) was prepared, separately. After 10 min (as vortexing time) their fluorescence spectra were recorded. In the Fenton reaction, hydroxyl radical (OH
) and oxidation form of used metal ion (Mn+1) can be produced. Among these ions, only simultaneous addition of Fe2+ and H2O2 could cause an obvious fluorescence quenching of the ACDs due to PET mechanism by the produced Fe3+ ions; (4) besides, the influence of Fe3+ and H2O2 on the ACDs fluorescence was also studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Fe3+ ions could be quenched ACDs fluorescence strongly (in comparison with hydroxyl radical). The presence of H2O2 or Fe2+ was almost unaffected on the ACDs fluorescence intensity. Also, the hydroxyl radical (produced in step 3) had a little effect on the fluorescence quenching of ACDs. Based on the results, a higher and more significant quenching in the fluorescence of ACDs occurred by Fe3+ ions in comparison to OH
. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ and OH
were estimated about 76% and 10%, respectively. Based on the Fenton reaction, Ni2+ and Co2+ ions in the presence of H2O2 can produce OH
radicals. By comparison of fluorescence quenching diagrams, we apperceived that quenching performance percentage of Ni2+ and Co2+ in the presence of hydrogen peroxide toward Fe3+ was significantly lower (about 8% and 10%, respectively), compared to Fe3+ alone. The small effect of highly reactive hydroxyl radical (produced of Fenton reaction) on the ACDs fluorescence, might be due to the oxidation of the surface groups, which finally lead to a gentle fluorescence quenching. Also, quenching performance percentage for Fenton system (Fe2+/H2O2) was about 53%. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity (F0 − F) and Fe3+ concentration with R 2 = 0.9926 was obtained by the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (5–300 µM) and LOD value the equal of 1.83 µM was calculated. In the oxidation reaction of glucose, glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzes the reaction between glucose and O2. Thus, gluconic acid and H2O2 are generated in this reaction. By adding of Fe2+ ions to this mixture, Fe3+ ions were produced and fluorescence quenching of ACDs taken place. Indeed, we developed an approach to detect glucose by the indirect production of hydrogen peroxide and following Fenton reaction. Electron transfer can be occurred between the ACDs (as electron acceptor or electron donor) and the quencher (as electron donor or electron acceptor). According to Scheme 1b, after photon-excitation of the metal–organic complex between ACDs (as electron donor) and Fe3+ (with d-orbital as electron acceptor), the complex would return to the ground state without emission of any photon. In Scheme 1b, the pathway (1) indicates the ACDs fluorescence emission after irradiation and pathway (2) demonstrates fluorescence quenching mechanism (PET mechanism) after complex formation between ACDs and Fe+3 ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of H2O2/Fe2+ molar ratio and time on the Fenton reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reagents molar ratio (H2O2 and Fe2+) and time factor on the Fenton reaction were optimized in our study. Fig. S2a shows that the maximum fluorescence quenching was obtained under the concentrations of 50 µM and 100 µM, respectively, for H2O2 and Fe2+ ions (molar ratio of 1:2). This molar ratio was adjusted in all over the next assays. Optimization of time for enzymatic process was carried out in the presence of constant glucose and GOx concentrations (500 μM, 3 U/mL) under the constant molar ratio of H2O2/Fe2+ (1:2). According to Fig. S2b, among the five tested time points (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 min), the time point of 30 min was the best incubation time, because maximum quenching of ACDs fluorescence took place at this time point (at 380 nm as the excitation wavelength). The fluorescence spectrum was recorded after adding ACDs to the mixture and was allowed 5 min for fluorescence quenching completion in the presence of produced Fe3+ from Fenton reaction.

As for enzymatic reactions, it can be expressed that using an additional amount of GOx will increase the experimental cost and the background signal. Therefore, we attempted to optimize the GOx concentration in the reaction at the next step of the experiment. At a constant incubation time and glucose concentration (30 min and 500 μM, respectively) and with the constant molar ratio of H2O2/Fe2+ (1:2), optimal GOx concentration was obtained by varying the GOx concentration (1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 5.0 U/mL). According to the Fig. S2c, fluorescence intensity of ACDs (at 380 nm as the excitation wavelength) was significantly dependent on GOx concentration. In this regard, the maximum ΔF (fluorescence quenching) was obtained by the application of 3 U/mL of enzyme, and so, this amount was considered as the optimal GOx enzyme concentration in this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs, as a sensor was investigated in a separate experiment. Due to the presence of –COOH groups on the ACDs surface, the proposed fluorescence probe was pH-dependent. In this stage, firstly, emission spectrum of ACDs selected as a blank in the absence of buffer. Then variations of fluorescence spectra of ACDs in the presence of two kinds of different buffers, Tris and Phosphate buffers (pH 2 to 11, 0.2 M), were recorded separately. Based on the results that shown in Fig. 4b, maximum fluorescence intensity of ACDs (in comparison with blank) was obtained in Tris buffer (pH = 4). Fig. S2d shows the effect of pH values (using Tris buffer, 0.2 M) on the fluorescence intensity of ACDs in the range of 2–11. These results showed that increase or decrease of pH could lead to protonation-deprotonation of carboxylic/hydroxyl groups and alteration of charges on the ACDs surface [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ACDs and studying interference effect of some metal ions and compounds (biomolecules)
ce:section.ce:para: Basically, selectivity is one of the important parameters for any sensing system. Briefly, the sensor response of ACDs that is based on the fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ ions, was investigated in aqueous solution in which the pH adjusted to lower than 7 to prevent ions precipitate to hydroxide form. Hereinafter, the same concentration (500 µM) of different metal ions (including Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+) was separately added to the ACDs solution (100 μL of ACDs stock solution in 5 mL of water) at the pH = 4. After an incubation time (about 5 min for every solution), fluorescence spectra were recorded under λ ex = 380 nm. The goal of this assay was the investigation of the fluorescence quenching of ACDs with Fe3+ ions in the presence of other ions (specially, Fe2+). Additionally, the impact of other compounds (such as sodium citrate (SC), potassium chloride (KCl), ascorbic acid (AA), lactic acid (LA), glucose (GL), uric acid (UA), (each to final concentration of 100 μΜ) on the ACDs fluorescence intensity in Fenton reaction was studied. Fig. 4c and 4d show interference effect of ions and biomaterial compounds on the ACDs fluorescence intensity, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor
Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor for Fe3+ ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: As another part of our work, the performance of designed nanosensor for Fe3+ ions determination was also investigated. In all of the steps, the various concentrations of Fe3+ solution (5, 10, 20, 50, 80, 150, 200, 300, 400 and 500 µM) were added to the constant concentration of ACDs solution (100 µL of stock solution). Then, each solution was allowed to mix well (for 1 min) and fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The linear plot was obtained by drawing (F0 − F) versus Fe3+ concentrations. F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of ACDs in the absence (as blank) and presence of the Fe3+ ions, respectively. Based on the results (see Fig. 5 a), the obtained linear range and LOD were 5–300 µM and 1.83 µM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ACDs-based sensor for H2O2 determination
ce:section.ce:para: ACDs were used as a fluorescence platform for hydrogen peroxide detection. For determination of H2O2 concentration by proposed sensor, sensing carried out in two steps: (1) firstly, a suitable concentration of ACDs (100 µL of stock solution) was prepared at Tris buffer (pH 4) with total volume of 5 mL and its fluorescence was recorded (at λ ex = 380 nm) as blank. The fluorescence intensity in this step is named “F0”. (2) The various concentrations of Fe2+ and H2O2 (in the ratio of 2:1) were prepared and solutions were incubated for 30 min until Fe3+ ions were generated on the base of Fenton reaction. Then, to the above-mentioned solutions (containing generated Fe3+ ions), the same concentration of ACDs (100 µL of stock solution) was added and pH was adjusted at optimum pH value and fluorescence was recorded after 5 min. In this step, fluorescence intensity is named ''F''. The quenching efficiency (QE) was calculated using the equations (ΔF = F0 − F or F0 − F/F) according to the previous studies [57]. According to the Fig. 5b, a good correlation observed between the quenching efficiency (ΔF) and the H2O2 concentration in the range of 10–200 μM using the following equation: (F0 − F) = 157.02 [H2O2] + 6126 (R 2 = 0.9989). By using the standard deviation of blank, Sb (with N = 10) and slope (m) of calibration plot of ΔF versus [H2O2], LOD (3Sb/m) was 975 nM for this sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the sensor for determination of glucose in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In this stage, GOx was used for specific catalyze of glucose in the presence of O2 to generate H2O2. Catalytic activity of GOx is pH-dependent [25], therefore, the pH of reaction was adjusted by adding Tris buffer (0.05 M, pH 7). The incubation time and GOx concentration were 30 min and 3 U/mL, respectively, as the optimal conditions for this reaction. The enzymatic reaction was performed in the room temperature [58]. By adding Fe2+ ions to the above solution, in the presence of H2O2, Fe3+ ions were produced according to the Fenton reaction. The Fe3+ ions quenched ACDs fluorescence and quenching intensity of the fluorescence positively correlated to the glucose concentration.

In this assay, calibration plot based on ΔF versus glucose concentration at 380 nm (as maximum excitation wavelength) was obtained (Fig. 5e). The regression equation was (F0 − F) = 50.188 [glucose] + 5886.7 with R 2 = 0.9906, and linear range of 5–500 µM with a limit of detection 2.84 µM. In this equation, F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of ACDs in the absence and presence of the reagents (GOx and Fe2+), respectively. The designed sensor was used to determine glucose in three samples including control serum (as standard), healthy and diabetic volunteers blood serums. In each sample, relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained for three replicate values. For the proposed sensor, the RSD of three replicate measurements for blood serum samples was obtained within the range of 0.25–0.75%. For evaluation of the results, the glucose content of the samples (without dilution) were also quantified by BT 3000 PLUS autoanalyzer for three replicated in a medical laboratory at different times. Before evaluation of glucose in real samples, by using proposed sensor, the samples were diluted 50-folds. The results of two assays were portrayed in the Table 1 . Generally, the RSD% results for blood serum samples by laboratory assay were higher than proposed assay. Meanwhile, the blood glucose concentration in healthy and diabetic person is about 3–8 mM and 9–40 mM, respectively [59,60]. The results indicate that the proposed sensor in this work is capable of analyzing the trace amount of glucose in human blood serum as a real sample. Eventually, the performance of the proposed sensor for glucose detection was compared with previously reports, which are listed in Table 2 .

Typical CDs was green synthesized by the biomass obtained from a microalga, Chlorella vulgaris as a precursor by hydrothermal method simply and successfully with smallish QY. Afterward, for analytical applicability of CDs, swelling microalgae were hydrolyzed by addition of dilute solutions of HCl and H2SO4 (which is the key step in here) and this mixture was ultrasonicated before hydrothermal step, until ACDs (with higher QY than CDs) were synthesized. Then, a selectivity study was performed for this sensing system and the ACDs properties were characterized and their analytical performance for Fe3+ ions, H2O2 and glucose detection (in real samples) was evaluated. Sensitivity, selectivity and dynamic range of proposed sensor, for glucose detection, were desirable than previously reports. Presumably, we can use ACDs in the field of nanomedicine in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant NO. 890412/3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.06.012.

ce:section.ce:para:
